does: This paper develops a multi-factor memory value function for LLM agents that must decide what to encode, forget, and retrieve under fixed memory budgets. The core contribution is a weighted combination of seven interpretable factors derived from cognitive psychology—emotional intensity, goal relevance, value alignment, self/user relevance, task utility, reliability, and usage history—whose weights are learned via gradient-free optimization from downstream task performance. A single scalar output from this value function uniformly controls encoding depth, forgetting risk, and retrieval ranking across the agent's interaction history. The work also makes a methodological point by demonstrating that standard evaluation on LongMemEval using semantic similarity to held-out questions conflates retrieval performance with forgetting quality, achieving approximately 0.98 retention of gold evidence but failing to measure principled forgetting in the realistic blind regime where future queries are unknown at consolidation time.
connects: This work builds on recent research into long-context LLM agents that must manage memory beyond transformer context windows, extending beyond simple heuristics like recency and semantic similarity that dominate current production systems. It connects to cognitive psychology literature on human memory consolidation and forgetting, particularly theories of what determines information retention in biological memory systems. The paper responds to limitations in existing memory management approaches for agents—which either require knowledge of future queries or use oversimplified retention criteria—by grounding the memory value function in established cognitive principles. It also engages with evaluation challenges in long-context understanding, building on work examining how semantic similarity metrics can give misleading signals about true memory performance, particularly distinguishing between retrieval and forgetting as separate problems.
enables: This approach enables more sophisticated memory management for long-running agentic systems that must operate under realistic constraints where future information needs are genuinely unknown at encoding time. The interpretable multi-factor framework allows inspection and debugging of what the agent considers valuable to remember, opening paths toward human-aligned memory consolidation in AI systems. The gradient-free learning mechanism suggests extensions to systems where direct backpropagation through memory operations is intractable. Future work could validate whether cognitive-psychology-grounded factors generalize across diverse agent tasks and domains, investigate learned weight distributions for interpretability, combine this approach with more sophisticated retrieval mechanisms beyond ranking, and develop better evaluation protocols that properly measure forgetting rather than retrieval under distributional shift.
